coredata.dc:title: NGQDs modified nanoporous TiO2/graphene foam nanocomposite for excellent sensing response to formaldehyde at high relative humidity
coredata.dc:creator: Shao, Shaofeng
Kim, Hyoun Woo
Kim, Sang Sub
Chen, Yunyun
Lai, Min
coredata.dc:description: In the vast majority of early breathing detection, nano-semiconductor gas sensors were widely used due to their high sensing activity and a relatively simple manufacturing process. We developed a simple post-synthetic hydrothermal treatment to fabricate a novel 3-dimensional (3D) structure gas micro-sensor, in which Au modified nanoporous N doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs)/TiO2 nanospheres were uniformly distributed throughout the graphene foam frameworks. The obtained graphene network-based nanoporous TiO2 gas micro-sensors with a high specific surface area provided a wealth of reaction sites for gas molecular diffusion and improve the response to target gas. The nanocomposites exhibited excellent gas-sensing performance toward ppb-level formaldehyde vapor by contrast gas detection, implying the application prospect in the aspect of breathing detection. More importantly, the graphene foam-based nanocomposites also presented outstanding selectivity and long-term stability. The excellent gas sensing properties were mainly attributed to the combination of NGQDs with TiO2 nanospheres, which indicated that the number of adsorbed oxygen and nano-heterojunction played an important role in enhancing the formaldehyde (HCHO) sensing performance of nanocomposites. Our work has updated a versatile synthesis strategy so that it can be programmed to design a broad series of nanoporous functional composites according to high sensing performance and general adaptability.

head.ce:title: NGQDs modified nanoporous TiO2/graphene foam nanocomposite for excellent sensing response to formaldehyde at high relative humidity

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Superior HCHO sensing performance has been realized on the new Au@NGQDs/TiO2/graphene foam gas sensor by virtue of introducing NGQDs functionalities in the TiO2 sensor.

In the vast majority of early breathing detection, nano-semiconductor gas sensors were widely used due to their high sensing activity and a relatively simple manufacturing process. We developed a simple post-synthetic hydrothermal treatment to fabricate a novel 3-dimensional (3D) structure gas micro-sensor, in which Au modified nanoporous N doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs)/TiO2 nanospheres were uniformly distributed throughout the graphene foam frameworks. The obtained graphene network-based nanoporous TiO2 gas micro-sensors with a high specific surface area provided a wealth of reaction sites for gas molecular diffusion and improve the response to target gas. The nanocomposites exhibited excellent gas-sensing performance toward ppb-level formaldehyde vapor by contrast gas detection, implying the application prospect in the aspect of breathing detection. More importantly, the graphene foam-based nanocomposites also presented outstanding selectivity and long-term stability. The excellent gas sensing properties were mainly attributed to the combination of NGQDs with TiO2 nanospheres, which indicated that the number of adsorbed oxygen and nano-heterojunction played an important role in enhancing the formaldehyde (HCHO) sensing performance of nanocomposites. Our work has updated a versatile synthesis strategy so that it can be programmed to design a broad series of nanoporous functional composites according to high sensing performance and general adaptability.

ce:section.ce:para: Because breathing diagnosis of cancer has the advantages of non-invasive, cheap, and easy access, it has a strong practical application prospect [1]. Especially when using portable and simple breath analyser for the detection of breath VOC gases, it is easy to screen the sick people. Formaldehyde (FA) is a carcinogen because it can induce intramolecular and inter-molecular cross-linkage between proteins and DNA, leading to the carcinogenesis. Indeed, the content of exhaled FA in lung cancer patients was higher than 83 ppb, while that in healthy patients was lower than 48 ppb. For these above reasons, more and more scientists were beginning to pay attention to the detection of FA. A series of physical or chemical methods, such as high-performance liquid chromatography, gas chromatography, fluorescence, calorimetry, and laser induced fluorescence were used to identify and detect FA content. However, instrumentation methods were not only expensive but also required a higher level of expertise. Therefore, it is very important to develop sensing materials with excellent sensing performance and to realize efficient and low-cost detection [2].

Compared to the conventional detect methods, semiconductor metal oxides (SMO)-based FA sensors have received considerable attention because they provided real-time measurements of rapid response and recovery for portable exhalation detectors. Many research efforts have focused on the enhancement of sensing performance of semiconductor metal oxide sensors. Different sensing materials with various nanostructured metal oxides have been reported, including nanorods, nanowires, nanobelts, nanoflowers, nanocombs, quantum dots, thin films, etc [3]. The ordered nanoporous materials with the high surface area promoted the interaction between the solid framework and the gas molecules compared to the bulk counterparts, which was highly advantageous for absorption-desorption and sensing processes involving surface reactions. At the same time, ordered nanoporous materials with interconnected channels and large pores facilitated gas diffusion, ensuring fast response and recovery with better sensitivity [4]. More recently, porous materials, especially three-dimensionally ordered macroporous (3DOM) materials, have indicated their potential applications in monitoring human health and detecting toxic gases due to their large surface area and many active sites by well-interconnected pore and wall structures [5].

It was well known that one of the main challenges of semiconductor metal oxide gas sensors was to improve selectivity. Many kinds of gases may trigger changes in the resistance of sensing materials, making it very difficult to accurately respond to specific volatile organic compounds (VOCs). Conventional enhancement ways to improve the selectivity of sensing materials mainly include adding dopant, forming composite materials, adjusting the working temperature and so on. Wang et al. synthesized Sn-doped 3DOM NiO for formaldehyde. They tested 10 at% Sn-doped 3DOM NiO for the detection of formaldehyde and reported a relatively good response (S = ~7 for 5 ppm at 225 °C). However, they did not report the measurement on the sensitivity to ppb-level FA [6]. Tian et al. reported a core-shell heterostructure for formaldehyde detection based on a combination of ZnO and ZIF-8. The selectivity of the ZnO@ZIF-8 nanorods sensor was improved for the detection of FA owing to the limitation effect of the aperture of ZIF-8 shell, but the sensitivity was still low [7]. Güntner et al. presented highly porous Pt-, Si-, Pd-, and Ti-doped SnO2 sensing films with high sensitivity for detecting FA. They revealed that porous sensing films had good sensing responses to ppb-level FA. However, the operating temperature of the gas sensor was as high as 400 °C [8]. In summary, although many achievements have been made in improving the selectivity of gas sensing materials, these sensors treated with different ways still have the problem of high operating temperature or low sensitivity for the ppb concentration of FA.

The three-dimensional defect-free graphene could provide a seamless continuous network with high intrinsic conductivity [9]. In addition, the porosity of this new graphene material offered the large specific surface area (up to ~850 m2 g−1) and was suitable for filling macropores with SMO materials to prepare functional composites [10]. In this work, a novel 3D graphene foam framework sensing material coated with Au modified nanoporous NGQDs/TiO2 nanospheres composite was fabricated by post-synthetic hydrothermal treatment. During the hydrothermal treatment, nanoporous TiO2 nanospheres were uniformly and firmly distributed throughout the surface of the graphene foam framework, which was never studied in previous reports for graphene foam-based sensing material. The interconnected graphene network facilitated fast electron transfer and the NGQDs also could facilitate fast charge transfer, increase the affinity between the nanoporous TiO2 nanospheres and the carbon-based framework, and promote the formation of p-n heterojunction to improve the sensing performance of composite materials. The results showed that introducing graphene foam to SMO sensors enhanced the response to low concentration FA, and at the same time reduced the response time to FA. Comparative research for HCHO gas detection was investigated to reveal the influence by combining with NGQDs on sensing materials. The results indicated that GF-NG-TA3 with 2 wt% NGQDs exhibited a high response of 6.1–40 ppb of HCHO at 150 °C. The high sensing performance obtained using our method could further enable potential applications in breath sensing detection.

3D-GFs were synthesized by chemical vapor deposition method using Ni foam as the growth substrate and ethanol as the carbon source [11]. The sensor device involves interdigitated-finger arrays and each finger is 10 μm in width and 1 mm in length. The finger-finger spacing is 8 μm, and with a finger-to-finger overlap of 800 μm, as shown in Fig. 1 a. Nickel foam (0.5 mm thick, 1.0 * 1.0 cm) cleaned with acetone were welded on the sensor device (Fig. 1b). For cleaning the nickel surface, the sensor device was exposed to 25 sccm H2 and 50 sccm Ar environment, and the temperature was raised with rate of 5 °C per minute up to 1000 °C, keeping for 15 min. Ethanol vapor was then introduced into the quartz tube by bubbling H2/Ar gas mixture at ambient pressure. After 25 min, the ethanol vapor was switched off and the system was cooled to room temperature. Then the nickel substrate was etched away with HCl solution at 90 °C to obtain 3D graphene foams, and cleaned with DI water and acetone for three times. Finally, the sensor device with 3D graphene foams was dried at 120 °C in the oven (Fig. 1c).

Preparation of Au nanoparticles was mainly based on the previous work [12]. Synthesis of NGQDs and 3D graphene-foam were shown in Supporting Information. The precursor solution was prepared by dissolving 1 g TiCl4 (Aldrich, >99%) in 5 ml ethanol (99.5%) in the presence of 5 mg, 10 mg, 20 mg, 40 mg NGQDs, respectively. 0.5 ml gold solution and was dropped in 0.2 ml 12 M HCl (37 wt%) with the aid of ultrasound for 10 min, and then dropped into the Ti precursor solution under constant stirring. The sensor device with graphene foam was immersed vertically into the Au-Ti precursor solution with 30 s and was lifted at a speed of 20 mm/min for four times. The sensor device was then dried at 60 °C for 30 min. The dried sensor device was exposed to a water vapor hydrothermal treatment, 95% relative humidity, at 150 °C for 72 h. The as-synthesized graphene foam-based composites were then heat treated at 573 K for 2 h under nitrogen atmosphere (Fig. 1d). According to the content of NGQDs, the samples are GF-NG-TA1, GF-NG-TA2, GF-NG-TA3, and GF-NG-TA4. In the process of sensing measurement, the liquid VOCs was inputted through a sample inlet and guided to the heater for evaporation, and the steam concentration was controlled by regulating the carrier gas flow rate and the speed and dosage of liquid VOCs injection. The gas sensing characteristics were carried out in a sample cell consists of a sample chamber with a gas inlet and outlet, which was introduced in ESI and the detail of the sample chamber shown on the ESI of previous literature [12].

The surface morphologies of the pristine 3D graphene foams were investigated by FE-SEM. Fig. 2 gives the FE-SEM images of the original graphene foams with different magnifications. As shown in Fig. 2a, we can see that the original graphene foams have a three-dimensional porous network structure with a pore size between 150 μm and 300 μm, which is significant for maintaining high adsorbate capacity. Fig. 2b exhibits the magnified images of the graphene foams, which demonstrates a smooth skeleton surface of the graphene foams.

3D graphene foam was further characterized by Raman spectra. As shown in Fig. 2c, two characteristic peaks at 1578 and 2683 cm−1 correspond to the G and 2D (G’) bands, respectively. Consistent with the SEM image, the highly symmetrical 2D (G’) band peak indicates that the skeleton surface of graphene foam is composed of multilayer graphene sheets. Furthermore, the absence of D band in the Raman spectrum of Fig. 2c indicates that 3D-GFs are of high quality with few defects [13]. Although the graphene layers are extremely thin, due to the absence of contact resistance between sheet junctions, the 3D-GF network possesses excellent mechanical strength and flexibility, and ensures high conductivity and stability [14]. The surface chemical properties of 3D-GFs were further characterized by the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). In Fig. 2d, the C1s XPS spectrum of 3D-GFs depicts two distinct components. The main peak at 284.6 eV corresponds to the non-oxygenated ring C, and the small peak reflects the existence of C in C-O bonds [15]. At the same time, XPS verified that trance elements of the catalyst and etching agents have been completely removed.

The influence of functionalized NGQDs on the crystallographic structures of the as-synthesized GF-NG-TA1, GF-NG-TA2, GF-NG-TA3, and GF-NG-TA4 samples was characterized by using WAXRD measurement (Fig. 3 ). For all samples, the diffraction peak of graphene foam can be found at 2θ = 26.4°. All diffraction peaks of GF-NG-TA1 pattern are found to match well with the anatase phase of TiO2, which is consistent with the standard card (JCPDS PDF 21-1272). These peaks observed at the diffraction angles (2θ) correspond to the crystal planes (1 0 1), (0 0 4), (2 0 0), (1 0 5), (2 1 1), and (2 0 4) of crystalline TiO2. On the other hand, from GF-NG-TA1 to GF-NG-TA3, the (0 0 4) diffraction peak become broader and the ratio intensity of I(2 0 0)/I(0 0 4) increases from 1.1 to 2.5, which indicates the massive appearance of (0 0 1) facets in GF-NG-TA3 [16]. For the GF-NG-TA3 sample, other characteristic peaks corresponding to the rutile phase of TiO2 are observed. Additionally, for the GF-NG-TA3 sample, the position of the (1 0 1) and (0 0 4) diffraction peaks shifts towards the lower angle (Fig. 3). This could be explained by the incorporation of NGQDs with the TiO2 nanoparticles [6]. Considering that the grain size of TiO2 nanoparticle in GF-NG-TA3 is smaller than that of GF-NG-TA1, this reduction in the particle size could be attributed to the inhibitory effect of crystal growth with the composition of NGQDs. Moreover, the gradual decrease of grain size is the result of lattice contraction caused by this reduction of lattice constant and leads to the formation of structural defects in GF-NG-TA3 sample [5].

For the GF-NG-TA4 sample, the intensity of the rutile phase increased as the concentration of the NGQDs was increased. Four main diffraction peaks at 2θ values of 27.5°, 36.2°, 41.4°, and 54.3° could be observed, corresponding to the (1 1 0), (1 0 1), (1 1 1), and (2 1 1) planes of rutile phase of TiO2 (JCPDS PDF 21-1276), respectively. The results clearly show that the phase of TiO2 could be modified by changing the concentration of NGQDs. The ratio of anatase and rutile phases determined from the WAXRD pattern was computed with the empirical relationship used by Depero et al. [17]

Among them, R(T) is the percentage of rutile content at a given temperature, and IA and IR are the main intensity of the diffraction peaks of anatase and rutile. The ratio of anatase phase to rutile phase in the different composites is 10:1 (GF-NG-TA2), 4.4:1 (GF-NG-TA3), 0.42:1 (GF-NG-TA4). Because the content of Au is low, no diffraction peaks corresponding to Au can be identified.

Herein, the influence of N doped graphene quantum dots for the local structure of the TiO2 matrix was also investigated by Raman spectroscopy. The comparison of the Raman spectra of GF-NG-TA1, GF-NG-TA2, GF-NG-TA3, and GF-NG-TA4 nanocomposites is shown in Fig. 4 . Three samples (GF-NG-TA1, GF-NG-TA2, GF-NG-TA3) display major anatase Raman bands at 155, 404, 516, and 636 cm−1, corresponding to the four Raman-active modes of the anatase phase with the symmetries of Eg(1), B1g, A1g, and Eg(2), respectively. Majumder et al. reported that the ration of the intensity of different Raman vibrational peaks (Eg and A1g) could give an idea about the exposed (0 0 1) facets of TiO2 nanostructures. The increase of the ratio intensity of Eg corresponding to the symmetric bending vibration of O-Ti-O bond and A1g peaks corresponding to the antisymmetric bending vibration of that indicates the higher percentage of exposed (0 0 1) facets [18]. In Fig. 4, the ratio of the intensity of Eg to A1g (IEg/IA1g) for GF-NG-TA1, GF-NG-TA2, and GF-NG-TA3 is 4.8, 5.1, and 7.8, respectively. The results demonstrate that the GF-NG-TA3 sample involves a much higher percentage of exposed (0 0 1) facets. When loading higher concentration of NGQDs on the GF-NG-TA4, rutile crystal peaks are observed at the characteristic Raman bands at 123, 249, 439, and 612 cm−1. In the case of the GF-NG-TA3 nanocomposite, all the Raman bands for anatase and graphene foam are still remained. Compared with the GF-NG-TA1 sample, the anatase Raman bands for GF-NG-TA3 nanocomposites are slightly broadened. Such a peak broadening is consistent with the decline of the average crystallite size. A broader band at around 1345 cm−1, called the D-band, is common for disordered sp2 carbon of the NGQDs with the small size. In the Raman spectra for GF-NG-TA3 nanocomposites, the two obvious peaks corresponding to graphene foam could still be observed. Two strong bands of graphene foam at around 1578 cm−1 and 2683 cm−1 are close to that observed for multilayer graphene and are often called as G-band and G’ mode. Also, with loading 2 wt% NGQDs on the nanocomposite, those two peaks become stronger. In addition, the peaks assigned to NGQDs within the nanocomposites become broadened and exhibit a slight blue-shift, signing a strong interfacial interaction between TiO2 and NGQDs. Such a result further features a real integration of the interface between graphene foam, NGQDs, and TiO2, rather than a simple mixture [19]. With loading 4 wt% NGQDs on the nanocomposite, the intensities of the peaks (155 and 636 cm−1) representing the anatase phase in the nanocomposites decrease to the weak state, and the intensities of rutile peaks at 439 and 612 cm−1 increase strongly as a result of phase transformation from anatase to rutile in the structure [20].

The porosity of graphene foam-based nanocomposites such as GF-NG-TA1, GF-NG-TA2, GF-NG-TA3, and GF-NG-TA4 were analysed by nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms. In Fig. 5 , all graphene foam-based nanocomposites depict typical IV isotherms, and the nanopore shape of NGQDs/TiO2 nanospheres is typically cylindrical. Obviously, with the different loading amount on the graphene foam, the hysteresis loops occur at different relative pressures, indicating the change of nanopore diameters, and the pore size distributions calculated by Non-local Density Functional Theory (NLDFT) method confirms this change. The average pore size of GF-NG-TA3 with one large hysteresis loop (P/P0 = 0.4–1.0) is 28 nm. For GF-NG-TA2 and GF-NG-TA4, the average pore sizes are 30 and 33 nm. So loading of NGQDs seems to partly affect the pore width distribution in the highly uniform pore structure [21]. The specific surface areas of GF-NG-TA1, GF-NG-TA2, GF-NG-TA3, and GF-NG-TA4 are 114.2, 110.9, 166.6, and 34.7 m2 g−1, respectively. The measurement found that the surface area of the GF-NG-TA3 sample is much higher than that of the other graphene foam-based composites. The high surface area of the GF-NG-TA3 sample is generally considered as an important factor in enhancing the electron transport and accelerating the gas diffusions as compared to its counterparts [22]. As discussed above, for the graphene foam-based nanocomposites, the loading of NGQDs has a significant effect on the surface areas and pore size distribution of the nanocomposites. The GF-NG-TA3 sample shows highest surface areas, best nanostructure, and narrowest nanopore distribution. Therefore, the study of GF-NG-TA3 nanocomposite is an essential part of the further investigation and discussion in the following section.

Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) was used to explore the morphologies of the nanocomposite after being coated with NGQDs/TiO2 nanospheres. The skeletons of the graphene foam were covered with NGQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites after post-synthetic hydrothermal treatment. In Fig. 6 a, the graphene foam maintains undamaged 3D porous structure after coated with NGQDs/TiO2 nanospheres during post-synthetic hydrothermal treatment. As shown in Fig. 6b, the skeleton surfaces of pristine graphene foams are completely covered by porous TiO2 nanospheres, and resulting in a micro-nanosized porous structure. The structure contains a large number of high-quality p-n heterojunctions, which could effectively increase sensing performance. Fig. 6c illustrates the magnified images of the coated graphene foams, further indicating that micro/nano porous structure derives from porous TiO2 nanospheres aggregated on the skeleton surface of the original graphene foam. Tight bonding and good monodispersity of nanoporous TiO2 nanospheres are essential for obtaining high sensitivity at low temperature [23]. The higher-magnification image of the NGQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites in Fig. 6d reveals hierarchical structures existing in the form of a micro-nano structure. This structure could dramatically boost the surface area and produce a composite interface in which target gas can be trapped within the grooves beneath the skeleton structure, thereby inducing super-sensitivity [24]. To further investigate the existence of Au and the dispersion of C, O, and Ti elements, we conducted EDS elemental mapping using FESEM (Fig. 6e–h). The results are found that the Au element is well-distributed in the GF-NG-TA3 nanocomposite, aiming to homogeneous sensitization both inside and outside of the skeleton structure. These images (Fig. 6f and g) indicate that porous TiO2 nanospheres are uniformly loaded on the skeleton of graphene foam.

The further exploration of the structural characteristics of the nanocomposite was carried out through TEM at different magnifications. The diameter of the nanosphere shown in the low magnification TEM image (Fig. 7 a) is in the range from 400 to 500 nm, and the corresponding high magnification TEM images (Fig. 7b) present that the hierarchical nanosphere consists of TiO2 nanoparticles and nanopores, which is benefit for gas diffusion, using as highly sensitive materials. The HRTEM images in Fig. 7c and d demonstrate typical TiO2 NPs with well-crystalline texture are tightly enwrapped by graphene sheets (<5 layers) with 0.373 nm average interlayer spacing. The presence of NGQDs in the nanospheres is further confirmed by the HRTEM image (Fig. 7d). The high magnification HRTEM image presents the crystalline interplanar spacing of 0.354 nm and 0.235 nm, corresponding to the (1 0 1) and (0 0 1) planes of tetragonal TiO2 in accordance with the anatase, respectively. The SAED pattern of the nanocomposite further certifies that the nanospheres are well crystalline, which is consistent with the WAXRD result above. It is proved that these nanospheres are composed of Au modified NGQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites. The unique coupled NGQDs/TiO2 heterojunctions provide more interface between TiO2 nanoparticles and NGQDs, which is conducive to more effective interfacial carrier transfer and sensing performance enhancement. In Fig. 7e–g, elemental mappings of the nanocomposite sphere were obtained for Ti, C, and Au, in which C and Au elements are regularly distributed throughout the hierarchical nanosphere. The uniform colour illustrates the homogeneous distribution of elements. Based on these observations it is concluded that NGQDs act as functional supports as well as a coordination-modulating agent for nanosphere growth.

Furthermore, XPS data provided more information on the surface composition and binding interactions of different species in GF-NG-TA3. Fig. 8 a–d show the high-resolution XPS spectra of Ti 2p, C 1s, O 1s, and N 1s regions, respectively. The Ti 2p region (Fig. 8a) depicts three peaks at 458.6 eV, 461.3 eV, and 464.4 eV. The peaks appearing at 458.6 eV and 464.4 eV could be assigned to Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 of TiO2, verifying the presence of TiO2 in respective GF-NG-TA3. Whereas, the peak appearing at 461.1 eV corresponds to the TiC bond confirming the embedment of TiO2 in the GF via Ti-C linkage [25]. Fig. 8b presents the high-resolution spectrum of the C 1s peak fitted into four peaks. The main peak at 284.8 eV is attributed to sp2-hybridized carbon (CC) from the carbon layers. The peak at 286.1 eV is originated from the hydroxyl carbon (CO), and the peak at 288.7 eV is assigned to carboxyl carbon (OC) or CN [26]. Moreover, the peak area at 284.8 eV of GF-NG-TA3 is larger than that of GF-NG-TA1, implying that there are more CC bonds on the surface of graphene foam after introducing higher content of N doped GQDs. The TiC peak located at 283.9 eV demonstrates that part of carbon has been successfully embedded into the lattice of TiO2 nanoparticles.

In Fig. 8c, the high-resolution spectrum of O 1s peak for GF-NG-TA3 shows that the peak could be fitted into four peaks, which are located at 529.7 eV, 530.6 eV, 532.1, and 533.4 eV, respectively. Binding energies at 529.7 and 530.6 eV correspond to lattice oxygen (Olatt) and surface adsorption oxygen species (Oads), respectively. The O 1s peak observed at 529.7 eV is assigned to the oxygen bonded with titanium. The surface adsorption oxygen usually plays an important role during HCHO detection at low temperature [27]. For GF-NG-TA1, the ratio of Oads/Olatt is less than 0.21. It is worth noting that the ratio of Oads/Olatt in TiO2 of GF-NG-TA3 is as high as 0.61, which will cause the high HCHO sensing activity. The other two peaks of GF-NG-TA3 at 532.1 eV and 533.4 eV are associated with the weakly oxygen-containing species, such as CO and CO groups [12].

Fig. 8d depicts the spectrum of N 1s for GF-NG-TA3 and GF-NG-TA4. For GF-NG-TA3, the peak at 399.8 eV is correlated to the electronic state of the nitrogen functional group: pyrrolic-N, and the peak at 398.3 eV is correlated to the nitrogen functional group: pyridinic nitrogen [28]. Pu et al. pointed that pyridinic nitrogen at the edge of carbon plane, where each nitrogen atom connects with two carbon atoms, contributes one p-electron to the aromatic π system, which has a significant effect on the electrical conductivity of nanocomposite [29]. Li et al. studied that some group locating at the edge of carbon lattices, such as pyridinic nitrogen, commonly presents higher activity [30]. From the results of GF-NG-TA3 and GF-NG-TA4 in Fig. 8d, it was found that the ratio of Npyridinic:Npyrrolic is 1.6 in GF-NG-TA3, and that of GF-NG-TA4 is only 0.3. The ratio change of different nitrogen functional groups is believed to have an effect on the related sensing reactions.

In this work, we synthesized the GF-NG-TA samples with hierarchical nanostructure and researched the effect of the composition of NGQDs on their gas-sensing properties. The hierarchical nanoporous structure and TiO2 crystal phase were modified by the improved post hydrothermal treatment. Compared with conventionally prepared TiO2 sensing materials, the sensing materials in this work was expected to offer more efficient gas sensing performance. On the other hand, the combination of N doped GQDs has also been proven to be a manageable and effective method to improve the sensing performance of semiconductor metal oxide sensors, because this combination could effectively regulate the crystal phases and oxygen adsorption.

Several factors such as operating temperature, sensitivity, selectivity, and stability could be of evaluating the sensing behaviour of these composite sensors. First of all, the operating temperature not only strongly affects the oxygen adsorption and desorption on the surfaces and interfaces of sensing materials but also controls the sensor response. The performance of the gas sensor after the 573 K calcination is shown in Fig. 9 a. 40 ppb concentration of HCHO is detected at different operating temperatures and 90% RH to determine the optimum operating temperature. It shows that from 50 °C to 150 °C the response increases with operating temperature and reach a maximum of 6.1 for GF-NG-TA3 at 150 °C, and then decreases with the further increase in temperature. Therefore, 150 °C is considered as the optimal operating temperature at which all other sensing measurements are carried out.

The typical response curves of GF-NG-TA1, GF-NG-TA2, GF-NG-TA3 and GF-NG-TA4 based gas sensors measured under various HCHO concentrations at 90% RH are shown in Fig. 9b. The HCHO gas exposure concentration was controlled between 40 ppb and 1000 ppb at 150 °C. When exposed to HCHO, the resistance of hierarchical nanoporous sensing materials decreased rapidly, showing the typical n-type sensing behaviour. Each response period was carried out within an exposure interval of 100 s, followed by another interval in dry air. With the increase of gas concentration from 40 ppb to 1000 ppb, the sensor response increased significantly. Among all the sensing materials, GF-NG-TA3 sample exhibited the best HCHO-sensing performance. As shown in Fig. 9b, the sensor response tracked the change of the HCHO concentration, and the response speed was extremely fast. After HCHO removal, the sensor could recover completely in a short time. Specifically, the response time was between 16 and 30 s and the recovery time was in the range of 18–32 s. It is a well-known strategy to improve the sensitivity of sensing materials that high-quality heterojunctions can be formed by the combination of hierarchical sensing materials with different concentrations of NGQDs to promote the catalytic decomposition of HCHO.

The sensor sensitivities of GF-NG-TA1, GF-NG-TA2, GF-NG-TA3 and GF-NG-TA4 samples as a function of HCHO concentrations are provided in Fig. 9c. The sensing response (S) of the GF-NG-TAx sensors can be empirically by the HCHO gas concentration (CHCHO) according to the following equation: S = αCHCHO β. Where α is the prefactor and β is the surface species charge parameter. As shown in Fig. S3, the linearity of S and HCHO concentration is fitted in a log-log coordinate system according to the following equation: Log(S) = logα + β logCHCHO [31]. The good linear relation in the concentration range from 40 ppb to 1000 ppb indicates that the sensor can meet the requirements of practical application. The adsorbed oxygen species could be evaluated based on β value. The β value for GF-NG-TAx (x = 1, 2, 3, 4) sensors are calculated to be 0.42, 0.49, 0.54, and 0.45, respectively, and the order of β value is GF-NG-TA3 > GF-NG-TA2 > GF-NG-TA4 > GF-NG-TA1. The β values are all relatively close to 0.5, which implies that the oxygen species absorbed on the TiO2 nanospheres is almost O2−. As the adsorbed O2− species is much more active and energetic than others (O2, O2 −, and O−), the large quantity of the adsorbed O2− may account for the high response of the as-prepared TiO2 nanoparticles [32]. For the GF-NG-TA3 sensor, as its β value is the largest, it shows the best sensing property. These results indicate that the concentration of NGQDs can effectively regulate the sensing performance.

Taking 40 ppb and 90% RH as an example, in Fig. 9d, GF-NG-TA3 sample exhibits a sensitivity of 6.1, which is more than three times higher than that (1.8) of GF-NG-TA2 sample. One probable reason is that the composition of NGQDs enhances the response of sensing materials, which is attributed to the heterojunction formed by NGQDs and TiO2 nanoparticles, and promotes the catalytic dissociation of HCHO molecules on the sensing materials at low temperature under the combined action of Au nanoparticles. When the amount of N doped GQDs continued to increase, the crystal phase of titanium oxide changed from anatase to rutile, and the heterojunction produced by N doped GQDs was also be changed, which affected the sensing performance. The ratio of anatase phase to rutile phase in the different composites are 4.4:1 (GF-NG-TA3) and 0.42:1 (GF-NG-TA4). Therefore, when the proportion of rutile phase with P-type semiconductor property was dominant, the performance of the sensor materials which originally exhibited N-type sensing performance would decrease significantly. Thus, the sensing response of GF-NG-TA4 was decreased to 1.5. It should also be pointed out that the GF-NG-TA3 sensor also possesses superior performance when compared with other semiconductor metal oxide-based sensors reported in previous works, which means that post-synthetic hydrothermal treatment is an effective method to synthesize high sensitivity 3D nanoporous TiO2 gas sensor with high-quality heterojunction of NGQDs and TiO2 nanoparticles and high effective combination between TiO2 nanospheres and graphene foam. A similar sensitivity could not be observed in the case of a similar hierarchical sensing materials prepared under similar conditions without NGQDs. For comparison, we examine the sensing performance of GF-TA (graphene foam-TiO2) (a), Au@GF-TA (Au@graphene foam-TiO2) (b), and GF-NG-TA3 (c), in terms of the HCHO sensing. The measurements are performed at 150 °C with HCHO gas exposure concentration of 40 ppb. The typical response curves of three different gas sensors being measured at an equal HCHO concentration are shown in Fig. S4. Pristine TiO2 material is not able to detect the ppb-level HCHO gas at low temperature. The GF-TA composite-based gas sensor presents an undoubted response to 40 ppb HCHO with a value of 0.35. If further decorated with Au nanoparticles, the Au@GF-TA based gas sensor improves the sensitivity double than that of GF-TA. Given sensors responses of all three tested materials, GF-NG-TA3 composite material displays the best HCHO-sensing performance, with six-fold higher sensor response to 40 ppb HCHO gas than the other two.

Selectivity is another important parameter for evaluating graphene foam-based nanocomposite gas sensors because poor selectivity can trigger false alarms and limit their practical utilization. Giannoukos et al. reported that VOCs in breath include isopropanol, benzene, ethanol, acetone, ammonia, and HCHO [33]. In this work, several vapor organic compounds were measured, and the sensitivity and response speed of GF-NG-TA3 sensor at 90% RH were discussed. The responses of the GF-NG-TA3 based gas sensors to eight target gases with the same concentration at 150℃ were further investigated, and the relevant results are shown in Fig. 9e. The present sensor displays quite outstanding selectivity to HCHO, and the responses reach 1.9, 1.6, 1.5, 1.4, 1.3, 0.7, and 0.3 to isopropanol, benzene, acetone, ethanol, aether, toluene, and NH3, respectively. The HCHO response becomes about 3 times as high as that to isopropanol. Thus, GF-NG-TA3 sample exhibits not only the high response but also high HCHO selectivity against isopropanol and other gases at a low gas concentration of 40 ppb.

In addition, in human breath, such as acetone, ethanol or NH3 are at high ppb level. Risby et al. reported that the concentration of acetone, ethanol or NH3 is much higher than HCHO, and the acetone has the greatest selective interference on the sensing materials [34]. Generally, acetone in the breath is about 200 ppb, if in the region where the sensitivity did not change anymore, which means that responses to the two-analyte mixture are distinguishable from each other as already expected for the sensor selectivity. In Fig. 9f, calibration curves of a GF-NG-TA3 sensor for formaldehyde detection (0–120 ppb) at higher acetone interferent levels ranging from 200 ppb to 600 ppb and 90% RH. Interestingly, these calibration curves with different interfering acetone levels are still similar to the curve without acetone. The sensitivity to FA of the individual sensors could not be significantly affected by the presence of acetone. When introducing less than 400 ppb of acetone, the sensitivity of the sensor is mainly attributed to the response to HCHO without acetone. This could indicate that acetone is disabled to make a competition for available adsorption sites and/or chemisorbed species on the GF-NG-TA3 surface. Interestingly, the sensitivity to FA still keeps stable, when the acetone level is further increased.

More evidence that the gas sensor in this work might have a potential application on breathing detection comes from studies of its good long-term stability. In Fig. S5, successive tests for the GF-NG-TA3 sensor are carried out toward 40 ppb HCHO at 150 °C. After 100 cyclic tests, the sensor still maintains its initial response. It proves the GF-NG-TA3 sample has excellent reproducibility. Moreover, as shown in Fig. S6, many repeated tests are conducted every 12 h, and the change of sensing response is less than 4.2% even after 10 days.

A comparison of the present work and reported sensing materials on sensing performance for HCHO detection is also shown in Fig. 10 and Table 1 . These reported sensing materials included MOS sensors (SnO2 microspheres, Sb-doped SnO2), and MOS composite sensors (In2O3/1%Co, 3DOM NiO/10%Sn, 5TiO2/Co3O4, ZnO@ZIF8). Wang et al. reported that GO/SnO2 NS-475 showed an extremely high-sensitivity to 100 ppb HCHO with the response value of 1.9 and a response time of 81.3 s at low temperature [35]. However, the sensitivity of the GO/SnO2 NS-475-based gas sensor decreased exponentially with low stability, when the operation temperature increased or decreased by 30 °C. At the operation temperature of 150 °C, the response value of GO/SnO2 NS-475 toward 100 ppb HCHO was less than 0.1. Ye et al. synthesized the rGO/TiO2 sensing film for detecting HCHO at low temperature [36]. The rGO/TiO2 gas sensor showed the sensitivity of 0.1 toward 100 ppb HCHO with a long response time of 320 s, which limited the application of the gas sensor in breath detection. From Fig. 10 and Table 1, comprehensively considering the sensitivity and response/recovery time, the GF-NG-TA3 sensor has possessed excellent sensitivity (9.1) toward 100 ppb HCHO at low temperature. It was worth mentioning that the GF-NG-TA3-based sensor also presented a fast response (18 s) and recovery speed (20 s) toward ppb-level HCHO at 90% RH. Therefore, excellent gas sensing characters of the GF-NG-TA3 based gas sensor ensure it has potential application in breathing detection.

In order to investigate the effect of relative humidity on the sensing properties to HCHO, the response characteristics of the GF-NG-TA3 sensor was evaluated under three different humidity level, i.e., 50%, 70%, and 90% RH. The controlled humidity environments were achieved by supersaturated salt aqueous solutions of different salts of Mg(NO3)2, NaNO3, KNO3 in the measurement chamber, respectively. Fig. S7 shows the gas response transient curves of the GF-NG-TA3 sensor at a working temperature of 150 °C for HCHO in 100 ppb under different humidity conditions. The sensing performance of the GF-NG-TA3 sensor has been reduced when the relative humidity is added from 50% to 90%, and the response values are 10.2, 9.6, and 9.1, respectively. Considering that the adsorption of water vapor can occupy the active adsorption sites of the sensing layers, the sensing performance declines at higher humidity levels. As the enhancement of the relative humidity, the sensing response time increases from 7 to 12 s. However, the recovery time is decreased from 26 to 13 s. The above results show that the GF-NG-TA3 sensor still exhibits fast response and recovery speed to the ppb-level concentration of HCHO at 90% relative humidity. Therefore, the functionalized GF-NG-TA3 sensor in this work has the potential application in human health breath analysis.

This work aims to synthesize the graphene foam substrate and then transform the substrate to the GF-NG-TA composites as the sensing materials. The fabrication process for producing GF-NG-TA composites is illustrated in Fig. 11 . Fig. 11a is the simulated image of the synthesized graphene foam substrate. The graphene foam was coated with porous TiO2 nanospheres, followed by functionalization with NGQDs. NGQDs/TiO2 nanospheres were covalently attached to the graphene foam and reduced through the calcination under the nitrogen atmosphere. When the amount of NGQDs was 0.5 wt% in GF-NG-TA1 sample, as shown in Fig. 11b, the main crystalline phase of titanium oxide was anatase phase; with the increasing concentration of NGQDs, the crystalline phase of titanium oxide gradually changed to rutile phase; when the amount of NGQDs was more than 4 wt% in GF-NG-TA4, as shown in Fig. 11d, the main crystalline phase of titanium oxide was rutile phase. The joint contribution of TiO2 nanospheres with 2 wt% NGQDs obtaining low surface free energy formed the basis for the high surface area of the resulting GF-NG-TA3 sensing materials. This process results in a transformation of the graphene foam to GF-NG-TA composites, which is ideal for use as excellent sensing materials [37].

The compositing process of Au@NGQDs and TiO2 nanoparticles not only affects the crystalline phase of TiO2 nanoparticles but the grain size of TiO2 nanoparticles, thus changing the resistance of sensing materials. As shown in the WAXRD pattern of GF-NG-Tas samples (Fig. 3), the grain size of TiO2 nanoparticle in GF-NG-TA3 is smaller than that of GF-NG-TA1 and GF-NG-TA2. In the GF-NG-TA3 based sensors, since the grain size (D ~ 3.5 nm) is smaller than L, where L is the width of the space charge region (that is Debye length, ~4.5 nm for TiO2). This reduction in the particle size could be attributed to the inhibitory effect of crystal growth with the composition of NGQDs. Wang et al. separately reported that the particle size of metal oxide materials had a great influence on the thickness of the electron depletion layer and diffusion of gas molecules during the gas sensing process [38]. Due to the chemisorption-induced oxygen surface states, the depletion layer would extend to cover the whole regions of the grains and enables a strong grain size effect, which means the resistance of GF-NG-TA3 would be higher than the other three sensors. Fig. S8 illustrates that the dynamic formaldehyde sensing response-recovery curves of different GF-NG-TA sensors with the concentration in the range from 40 ppb to 1000 ppb at 150 °C. With the increment of NGQDs, the resistances of different sensing materials at air are 390MΩ, 540MΩ, 660MΩ, 610MΩ for GF-NG-TA1, GF-NG-TA2, GF-NG-TA3, and GF-NG-TA4, respectively. The initial resistance (Ra) value of the GF-NG-TA3 sensor is larger than that of the other sensors. This indicates that the electron-depletion layer of the GF-NG-TA3 sensor is further expanded by the creation of NGQDs-TiO2 p-n heterojunctions. For the GF-NG-TA3 sensor, the resistances of the sensing materials reduce rapidly after exposure to formaldehyde at different concentrations and can recover to the original resistance after reduplicate exposure to air.

The high resistance of GF-NG-TA3 in the air is also due to the high content of chemisorbed oxygen molecules on the surface of sensing materials. Chemisorbed oxygen molecules in the atmosphere are ionized to oxygen ions through the capture of free electrons from the surface of Au@NGQD/TiO2 heterojunctions. The XPS analysis indicated that combining of Au@NGQDs to form Au@NGQDs/TiO2 heterojunctions would induce abundant adsorbed oxygen. For the GF-NG-TA1 in Fig. 8c, the ratio of Oads/Olatt is less than 0.21. The ratio of Oads/Olatt in TiO2 of GF-NG-TA3 is as high as 0.61, which is higher than the other three sensors. By effectively compounding Au with NGQDs, a better catalyst for oxygen dissociation catalyst than TiO2 improved the rate of dissociation and diffusion of oxygen species on the surface of TiO2, resulting in a greater degree of electron withdrawal from the conduction band of TiO2 (“spill-over” effect) [39]. Fig. 12 presents the sensing mechanism of the sensing material in the air and HCHO vapor. During the exposure of the GF-NG-TA3 sensor in the air, a large number of oxygen molecules would be adsorbed and diffused on the interface of Au@NGQDs/TiO2 heterojunctions and captured plenty of electrons from the conduction band of TiO2 through the above heterojunctions to form chemisorbed oxygen species (O2−). Those chemisorbed oxygen species and the possible reaction to HCHO could be described as Eqs. (1)–(3):

In the Au@NGQDs/TiO2 heterojunctions (Fig. 12), the work function of Au (5.1 eV) is larger than that of anatase (3.2 eV) and rutile (3.0 eV). Similar to the NGQDs/TiO2 heterojunctions, the Au@NGQDs/TiO2 heterojunctions would provide additional modulation of resistance during the adsorption of oxygen molecules by changing the heterojunction barrier. The carrier concentration on the surface of sensing materials decreased rapidly and the resistance increased quickly, as shown in Fig. 12a. As shown in the energy band diagram (Fig. 12c), in the Au@NGQDs/TiO2 heterojunction region, the band bending was upward. Therefore, a thicker electron depletion layer was formed near the surface of the TiO2 nanosphere. When the GF-NG-TA3 sensor was exposed to the HCHO gas, HCHO molecules would react with the adsorbed oxygen species, releasing a large number of electrons in the heterojunction region and re-transferring these electrons to the conduction band of TiO2. The surface of the sensing materials was covered with lots of electrons, the surface resistance would drop quickly, and the thickness of the electron depletion layer would become thinner rapidly. As could be seen from Fig. 12b, Au@NGQDs/TiO2/GF nanocomposite sensor presents far greater sensitivity to HCHO gas than other gases, showing the excellent selectivity to HCHO. As shown in the energy band diagram (Fig. 12d), in the Au@NGQDs/TiO2 heterojunction region, the band bent downward.

GF-NG-TA3 sensor could provide abundant reaction sites, accelerated the surface reaction speed, and enhanced the reaction ability of the sensing material. In the Raman spectra of GF-NG-TA3 (Fig. 4), the ratio of the intensity of Eg to A1g (IEg/IA1g) for GF-NG-TA3 is 7.8, which demonstrates that the GF-NG-TA3 sample involves a much higher percentage of exposed (0 0 1) facets than the other sensors. Liu et al. investigated the adsorption mechanisms of HCHO on TiO2 surfaces and found that the anatase (0 0 1) surface was more active in HCHO adsorption with lower adsorption energy and larger charge transfer [40]. Wang et al. combined kinetic and theoretical methods to examine the reaction of HCHO on the TiO2 surface and found that the preeminent role of entropy underpins the much higher reactivity of HCHO than the other target gases on the TiO2 surfaces at relevant temperatures [41]. For the GF-NG-TA3 sensor with the highest percentage of (0 0 1) facet, this facet plays an important role in improving the gas-sensing properties of the GF-NG-TA3 sensor for keeping the stability of it. In this work, the sensing system is relatively complex, and the subsequent work will take a more in-depth study on the sensing mechanism in the future.

In the overwhelming majority of early breath detection, nanosized semiconductor gas sensors were widely used due to the high sensing activity and relative simplicity of the manufacturing process. In this work, we have developed a simple post-synthetic hydrothermal treatment to prepare the new three-dimensional gas sensor, in which Au modified nanoporous NGQDs composite TiO2 nanospheres were evenly distributed on the entire surface of the graphene-foam frame. The obtained graphene network-based nanoporous TiO2 gas micro-sensors had a high specific surface area that provided abundant reaction sites for gas molecular diffusion and that improved the response-ability of the gas sensor. The comparative research for HCHO gas detection were investigated to reveal the influence through combining with NGQDs on sensing materials. GF-NG-TA3 sensor with the highest percentage of (0 0 1) facets exhibited the highest sensitivity to ppb-level HCHO at high relative humidity. The measurement results indicated that GF-NG-TA3 with 2 wt% NGQDs exhibited a high response of 6.1 to 40 ppb of HCHO at 150 °C. More importantly, the graphene foam-based nanocomposites also presented outstanding selectivity and long-term stability. The combination of Au, NGQDs, and TiO2 nanospheres was the main reason for the excellent gas sensing performance. It was indicated that the amount of oxygen vacancies, adsorbed oxygen and nano-heterojunction played an important role in enhancing the HCHO sensing performance of nanocomposites. In addition, our work has since updated a versatile synthesis strategy, so that it can be programmed to design a broad series of nanoporous functional composites according to high sensing performance and general adaptability.
